slides.title: Strings are Objects 🧵
slides.content:
Remember how `String` starts with a capital 'S'? That's because it's not a primitive. It's a complex Object.

And objects have their own **Methods** built right into them!

You can access these cool text-manipulation abilities by putting a dot `.` after your string variable.

slides.title: The Big 3 🧵
slides.content:
There are dozens of string methods, but here are the three most common ones you'll use constantly:
1. `.length()` -> Returns how many characters long it is. (Note: It has parentheses unlike Array length!)
2. `.toUpperCase()` -> Returns an all-caps version.
3. `.equals("...")` -> Used to verify if two strings are identical.

quizData.question: Why shouldn't you use `==` to compare two Strings? e.g. `if (word == "Hello")`

options.text: Because Strings are Objects. == compares their memory addresses, while `.equals()` compares their actual text characters.
It is perfectly fine to use ==.

quizData.explanation: With complex Objects like Strings, `==` asks 'Are these the exact same item in RAM?'. `.equals()` asks 'Do these two different items contain the same text?'. ALWAYS use `.equals()` for Strings.
